skillName: Area of rectangles, parallelograms, triangles and compound shapes
misconceptionSummary: Students can't see why the triangle formula has a half and why a parallelogram equals a rectangle of the same base and height.

visuals.ruleText: A parallelogram can be cut and rearranged into a rectangle of the same base and height
visuals.subText: Slice the triangle from one end and attach it to the other — same area, new rectangle

steps.narration: Cut a right-triangle from one end of a parallelogram and slide it to the opposite end. The shape becomes a rectangle — same base, same perpendicular height, same area. That is why both use base times height.

lines.text: Two identical triangles form a parallelogram

steps.narration: Every triangle is exactly half a parallelogram — place two identical triangles together and they form a parallelogram. So a triangle's area is exactly half of base times height.

lines.text: Split compound shape into rectangles and triangles

steps.narration: Compound shapes are handled by splitting. Identify rectangles and triangles within the compound shape, calculate each area separately, and add — or subtract cut-out regions.

visuals.expression: Triangle = ½ × parallelogram = ½ × b × h
visuals.label: Two triangles make a parallelogram — hence the half

steps.narration: The half in the triangle formula is not arbitrary — it directly reflects that a triangle is half a parallelogram. The visual proof makes it memorable.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: forgetting the half in the triangle formula. Two identical triangles tile a parallelogram — so one triangle is exactly half the base × height product.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: A triangle is half a parallelogram — join two together and they form one. That is where the half in the formula comes from. It is not arbitrary.
